metadata.name: 7th Grade School Curriculum
metadata.description: Questions and concepts from the 7th grade curriculum — world geography, world history, life science, algebra, art history, and health.
metadata.author: Trivial World Team

questions.questionText: What is the largest continent in the world by area?
What is the capital of France?
The Amazon River, the world's largest river by volume, flows through which country?
What mountain range stretching over 4,300 miles forms the natural spine of South America?
What large island nation off the southeast coast of Africa is famous for unique animals like lemurs found nowhere else on Earth?
Which country has the longest coastline in the world?
On which continent is the Sahara Desert located?
questions.tidbits: The Sahara is the world's largest hot desert, spanning about 3.6 million square miles.

questions.questionText: What is the capital of Australia?
questions.tidbits: Many people guess Sydney or Melbourne, but Canberra was purpose-built as the capital in 1913.

questions.questionText: What is the name of the narrow strip of land that connects North America and South America?
Which ocean is the smallest in the world?
What is the capital of China?
What is the largest country in South America by land area?
What sea lies between Europe and North Africa, bordered by Spain, France, Italy, and Greece?
Which river, running through Egypt, is the longest river in Africa?
What is the term for an imaginary line running from the North Pole to the South Pole that divides Earth into eastern and western halves?
Which Asian country has the largest population in the world?
What is the name of the imaginary line at 0 degrees latitude that circles the middle of the Earth?
What is the name of the world's largest desert, covering most of Antarctica?
What type of government is it when a single person has complete, unlimited power over a country?
What is the name of the large island that is the world's largest, located between the Arctic and Atlantic Oceans near Greenland?
Who was the first person to walk on the Moon?
Who wrote "Romeo and Juliet"?
What civil rights leader delivered the "I Have a Dream" speech in 1963?
What is the name of the holy book of Islam?
What is the first name of the narrator of S.E. Hinton's novel "The Outsiders"?
Which ancient civilization built the pyramids at Giza?
What famous explorer, sailing for Spain, first reached the Caribbean in 1492?
The "Silk Road" was an ancient network of trade routes connecting Europe with which region?
questions.tidbits: The Silk Road also carried religion, disease, and culture — not just silk.

questions.questionText: What literary device gives human qualities to non-human things, such as "the wind whispered"?
What Spanish conquistador conquered the Aztec Empire in Mexico during the 1520s?
Who is the protagonist of Harper Lee's novel "To Kill a Mockingbird"?
What is the name of the religious text central to the Jewish faith?
Who wrote 'The Outsiders,' becoming one of the youngest published novelists in American history?
What is the term for a comparison using 'like' or 'as,' such as 'fast as lightning'?
What ancient Greek city-state was famous for its warrior culture and militaristic training program for boys?
Who was the Egyptian queen who ruled alongside Julius Caesar and later Mark Antony?
What famous conqueror from Macedonia built one of the largest empires in history by age 30?
What Greek philosopher was the teacher of Alexander the Great?
What is the main conflict in S.E. Hinton's 'The Outsiders' about two rival social groups?
questions.answerText: The rivalry between the Greasers and the Socs

questions.questionText: What is the name of the long poem attributed to Homer that describes the fall of the city of Troy?
What period of cultural rebirth in Europe, beginning in Italy in the 14th century, is known for its art and learning?
What deadly disease, spread by fleas on rats, killed about one-third of Europe's population in the 14th century?
What battle in 1066 resulted in the Norman conquest of England?
What social system of medieval Europe involved lords granting land to knights in exchange for military service?
What did Johannes Gutenberg invent in the 1440s that transformed the spread of knowledge across Europe?
In what century did Gutenberg invent the printing press? A) 13th, B) 14th, C) 15th, D) 16th
What series of medieval Christian military expeditions were launched to capture Jerusalem from Muslim rulers?
What ancient document, signed by King John of England in 1215, established that even rulers must obey the law?
questions.tidbits: Magna Carta means "Great Charter" in Latin and is one of the first documents to limit royal power.

questions.questionText: What is the term for the period of intense scientific discovery in the 16th and 17th centuries, when thinkers like Galileo and Newton changed our understanding of the universe?
What Portuguese explorer, sponsored by the Spanish crown, led the first expedition to circumnavigate the globe (though he died before completing it)?
What empire, ruled from Rome, controlled most of Europe and the Mediterranean for centuries before falling in 476 AD?
What ancient civilization built the Great Wall to protect its northern border?
What was the name of the ancient Egyptian writing system that used pictures and symbols?
What ancient Greek city-state is known as the birthplace of democracy?
What Roman leader was assassinated on March 15, 44 BCE — a date known as the Ides of March?
What religious movement in the 16th century challenged the Catholic Church's authority and led to the creation of Protestant churches?
What ancient trade route connected China to the Mediterranean world, carrying silk, spices, and ideas?
What is the term for the spread of Greek culture, language, and art following Alexander the Great's conquests?
What document, issued by English King John in 1215, limited royal power and is considered a forerunner of constitutional government?
What was the name of the Aztec capital city, built on an island in a lake, that became modern-day Mexico City?
What Italian artist painted the ceiling of the Sistine Chapel?
What literary device is it when multiple consecutive words start with the same sound, like "Peter Piper picked pickled peppers"?
Who painted the Mona Lisa?
What art movement, developed in France in the 1860s, is known for capturing light using small, visible brushstrokes?
What Spanish artist, who co-founded Cubism, painted the anti-war mural "Guernica"?
What literary device involves a reference to another work of literature, history, or culture?
Which Dutch master of the 17th century painted "The Night Watch," one of the most famous paintings in Western art?
What is the Japanese art of flower arrangement called?
What is the term for a 14-line poem with a specific rhyme scheme, of which Shakespeare wrote 154?
What Japanese master artist created "The Great Wave off Kanagawa," one of the most recognized images in art history?
What is the name of the art technique where an artist carves into a block of wood or stone and presses it onto paper to make prints?
What Dutch painter is famous for painting bright sunflowers in a vase?
What literary device is the use of a word that imitates the sound it describes, like 'buzz' or 'crash'?
What French artist is known for painting ballet dancers in his signature Impressionist style?
What is the term for a direct comparison of two unlike things that does NOT use 'like' or 'as'?
What famous Mexican painter, known for self-portraits and vibrant folk art imagery, was married to muralist Diego Rivera?
What is the term for the technique of creating a mosaic image in digital art by arranging small colored dots or pixels?
questions.answerText: Pixel art (or pointillism in traditional art)

questions.questionText: What is the name of the ancient Greek temple on the Acropolis of Athens, dedicated to the goddess Athena?
What is the name of the literary technique where a narrator tells a story from outside the events, referring to characters as 'he,' 'she,' or 'they'?
What is the art of cutting and folding paper into shapes, associated with the Japanese cultural tradition?
What gas makes up the largest portion of Earth's atmosphere? A) Oxygen, B) Carbon dioxide, C) Nitrogen, D) Hydrogen
questions.tidbits: Nitrogen makes up about 78% of our atmosphere; oxygen is about 21%.

questions.questionText: What is the basic unit of all living things?
What is the value of x if 2x = 14?
What is the chemical formula for water?
questions.answerText: H₂O (two hydrogen atoms bonded to one oxygen atom)

questions.questionText: What process do plants use to convert sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide into glucose?
What is the difference between potential energy and kinetic energy?
questions.answerText: Potential energy is stored energy (energy of position or condition); kinetic energy is the energy of motion

questions.questionText: What is the term for a pure substance that cannot be broken down into simpler substances by chemical means?
What branch of science studies the composition, structure, and properties of matter and how substances interact?
If a triangle has two angles measuring 40° and 65°, what is the measure of the third angle?
questions.answerText: 75° (all angles of a triangle add up to 180°)

questions.questionText: What is the key difference between mitosis and meiosis?
questions.answerText: Mitosis produces 2 identical daughter cells for growth and repair; meiosis produces 4 genetically unique cells for sexual reproduction

questions.questionText: What is the value of x if x + 5 = 12?
What organelle in a plant cell contains chlorophyll and performs photosynthesis?
What is the term for the number of times an event occurs divided by the total number of trials, used in probability?
What is the term for an organism that makes its own food through photosynthesis or chemosynthesis?
What is the value of 3² + 4²?
What type of reproduction involves only one parent and produces genetically identical offspring?
What is the equation for calculating speed?
What is the term for the sequence of energy transfers from producers to consumers in an ecosystem?
In algebra, what is the value of y if 3y − 6 = 9?
What is the term for the layer of cells that surrounds and protects a plant cell?
How many bones are in the adult human body? A) 106, B) 206, C) 306, D) 406
What body system pumps blood through the heart and blood vessels to deliver oxygen to cells?
What organs in the body filter waste from blood to produce urine?
How much moderate physical activity per day do health guidelines recommend for children and teens? A) 15 minutes, B) 30 minutes, C) 60 minutes, D) 90 minutes
In basketball, what is the violation called when a player moves with the ball without dribbling?
Which macronutrient provides the most energy per gram — fats, proteins, or carbohydrates?
questions.answerText: Fats (9 calories per gram, versus 4 calories per gram for both proteins and carbohydrates)

questions.questionText: In American football, how many points is a field goal worth? A) 1, B) 2, C) 3, D) 6
What system of muscles and bones work together to support the body and enable movement?
What is the difference between aerobic and anaerobic exercise?
questions.answerText: Aerobic exercise uses oxygen for sustained activity (like jogging); anaerobic exercise doesn't rely on oxygen and is used for short, intense bursts (like sprinting)

questions.questionText: What combined track-and-field event tests athletes in 10 disciplines over two days?
What is the largest organ in the human body?
How many players are on each side in a volleyball game?
What is the function of the respiratory system?
questions.answerText: To bring oxygen into the body and remove carbon dioxide through breathing

questions.questionText: What is the name of the nutrient that builds and repairs muscle tissue?
What is the penalty in soccer when a defensive player commits a foul inside their own penalty area?
What is the name of the flexible connective tissue that cushions the ends of bones at joints?
What is the term for a goal in ice hockey scored when the puck passes the opposing team's goalie?
What is the name of the physical fitness test that measures how long a person can run back and forth between two lines before failing to reach a line in time?
What type of joint, found in the shoulder and hip, allows movement in multiple directions?
What is the term for the process by which the body converts food into energy that cells can use?
